column1: description

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function waits for a callback to be executed on the audio thread, but it does not check if the audio thread is actually running before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a deadlock if the audio thread is not running, causing the application to hang indefinitely.

The vulnerability is in the function fz_new_image_from_buffer in libfreetype.so.2.5.2. It does not check if the buffer size is sufficient to hold the image data, which can lead to a buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a crafted buffer size to cause a buffer overflow and execute arbitrary code.

The Android kernel in Android 4.x before 4.4.4 allows local users to gain privileges by modifying the /data/tombstones directory, which can be used to write arbitrary files, aka internal bug 26403620.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function waits for a callback to be executed on the audio thread, but it does not check if the audio thread is actually running before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a deadlock if the audio thread is not running, causing the application to hang indefinitely.

The vulnerable C code snippet in libimageworsener.a in ImageWorsener before 1.3.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted image, related to imagew-bmp.c and imagew-util.c.

The vulnerable C code snippet in libimageworsener.a in ImageWorsener before 1.3.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted image, related to imagew-bmp.c and imagew-util.c.

The vulnerability is in the function jffs2_get_acl() in the kernel module jffs2. The function does not check if the /data/tombstones directory exists before attempting to read the ACLs from it, which can lead to a NULL pointer dereference and a potential crash. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by creating a file with a crafted name that contains a null byte, which can cause a buffer overflow and lead to a crash.

The vulnerability is in the function walk_string() in libfreetype.so.2.5.2. It does not check if the input string is NULL before accessing it, which can lead to a segmentation fault. An attacker can provide a NULL input string to this function and cause a segmentation fault.

The vulnerability is in the function build_l4proto_dccp() in the Linux kernel before 4.12.10. It does not check if the ATTR_DCCP_STATE attribute is set before accessing it, which can lead to a NULL pointer dereference and potential crash. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a crafted packet to trigger a NULL pointer dereference.

The vulnerability is in the function strupper_talloc_n_handle in libsmbclient.so in Android 4.x before 4.4.4. It does not check if the /data/tombstones directory exists, which allows local attackers to gain privileges by creating a symlink to a directory outside of the intended directory.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function does not check if the `use_audio_thread_` variable is set to true before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`, which can cause a deadlock if the `use_audio_thread_` variable is false and the audio thread is not running. This can lead to a denial of service or other issues.
The Android kernel before 4.4.4 allows local users to gain privileges via a crafted application, aka internal bug 26403620.

The vulnerability is caused by a lack of input validation in the function build_l4proto_dccp, which allows an attacker to cause a buffer overflow by sending a crafted packet with a large value for the DCCP_STATE or DCCP_ROLE field. This can lead to a denial of service or possibly other unspecified impact.

The zsetstrokecolor function in zpl.c in Ghostscript before 9.18 does not properly handle the case where the color is not set, which allows attackers to bypass intended security restrictions via a crafted PDF file, aka unspecified vulnerability.

The vulnerability is in the function build_l4proto_dccp in the kernel module nf_conntrack_proto_dccp.c. It does not check if the port number is within the valid range before setting it in the struct nfct_attr_grp_port. This allows an attacker to set an invalid port number, which can cause a buffer overflow and lead to a denial of service or other issues.

The Android kernel before 4.4.4 allows local users to gain privileges by creating a directory with the name "tombstones" in the /data directory, which can be used to write arbitrary files to the /data/tombstones directory.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function is used to wait for a callback to be executed on the audio thread. However, it does not check if the audio thread is already running before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a deadlock if the audio thread is not running, as the function will wait indefinitely. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by calling `WaitForCallback()` before starting the audio thread, which can cause a denial of service or crash.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function does not check if the `use_audio_thread_` variable is set to true before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`, which can lead to a potential crash or hang if the variable is not set. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by setting `use_audio_thread_` to false and then calling `WaitForCallback()` to cause a deadlock.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in libimageworsener.a, which does not check if the `use_audio_thread_` variable is set before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a potential crash or hang if `use_audio_thread_` is not set.

The fht16x16_ref function in libvpx/vp9/vp9_rtcd.h in Google VPX before 1.6.0 does not properly handle the input data, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (buffer over-read) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted input, related to vp9_fht16x16_c.

The vulnerability is in the function compare_img() in libvpx.a in VPX before 1.6.0. It does not check if the memory pointed by img1 and img2 is valid, which can lead to a buffer overflow and a potential security issue. An attacker can provide a crafted image to cause a buffer overflow and execute arbitrary code.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in libimageworsener.a, which does not check if `use_audio_thread_` is true before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a potential crash or hang if `use_audio_thread_` is false.

The vulnerability is in the function `WaitForCallback()` in the ImageWorsener library. The function waits for a callback to be executed on the audio thread, but it does not check if the audio thread is actually running before calling `base::RunLoop().RunUntilIdle()`. This can lead to a deadlock if the audio thread is not running, as the function will wait indefinitely.

The fht8x8_ref function in libvpx/vp9/common/postproc.c in Google VPX before 1.6.0 does not properly handle the case where the input data is not aligned, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (buffer over-read) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted input, related to postproc_down_ac.c.

The vulnerable C code snippet in the function CheckSad() in the file init.rc in Android 4.x before 4.4.4 does not ensure that the /data/tombstones directory exists for the Debuggerd component, which allows attackers to gain privileges via a crafted application.

The vulnerable C code snippet in libimageworsener.a in ImageWorsener before 1.3.1 has a left shift undefined behavior issue, which might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted image.

The vulnerable C code snippet in libimageworsener.a in ImageWorsener before 1.3.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted image, related to imagew-bmp.c and imagew-util.c.

The vulnerability is in the function build_l4proto_dccp() in the Linux kernel, which does not check if the port number is within the valid range before setting it in the netfilter connection tracking attributes. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by sending a crafted packet with an invalid port number.

The vulnerability is in the function variance_ref in libimageworsener.a in ImageWorsener before 1.3.1. It does not check if the input parameters are within the expected range, which can cause a buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a crafted image file that can cause a denial of service or crash the application.
The Android kernel before 4.4.4 allows local users to gain privileges by modifying the /data/tombstones directory, which is used by the Debuggerd component, via a symlink attack.
